subject: Physics
q: A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is placed on a surface with a vertical cross section given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mn>6</mn></mfrac></math>. If the coefficient of friction is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math>, the maximum height above the ground at which the block can be placed without slipping is
question: A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is placed on a surface with a vertical cross section given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mn>6</mn></mfrac></math>. If the coefficient of friction is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math>, the maximum height above the ground at which the block can be placed without slipping is
solution:
For block remain stationary.

μ=tanθ (θ is angle at that point)

∴tanθ=μ=12(slope)

y=x36

∴dydx=3x26=x22⇒slope=12⇒x=1

∴y=(1)36 =16 m

subject: Physics
q: A coin placed on a rotating table just slips when it is placed at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the centre. If the angular velocity of the table is halved, it will just slip when placed at a distance of _____ from the centre:
question: A coin placed on a rotating table just slips when it is placed at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the centre. If the angular velocity of the table is halved, it will just slip when placed at a distance of _____ from the centre:
solution:
The coin will slip when 

μmg=mω2r

⇒μg=ω2×1 cm   ...(i)

Now, ω'=ω2

μg=ω22R   ...(ii)

Hence we can write from (i) and (ii)

ω2×1 cm=ω24R⇒R=4 cm

subject: Physics
q: A given object takes $\mathrm{n}$ times the time to slide down $45^{\circ}$ rough inclined plane as it takes the time to slide down an identical perfectly smooth $45^{\circ}$ inclined plane. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the object and the surface of inclined plane is :
question: A given object takes $\mathrm{n}$ times the time to slide down $45^{\circ}$ rough inclined plane as it takes the time to slide down an identical perfectly smooth $45^{\circ}$ inclined plane. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the object and the surface of inclined plane is :
solution: Case-1 : No friction $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{a}=\mathrm{g} \sin \theta \\ & \ell=\frac{1}{2}(\mathrm{~g} \sin \theta) \mathrm{t}_1^2 \\ & \mathrm{t}_1=\sqrt{\frac{2 \ell}{\mathrm{g} \sin \theta}} \end{aligned}$ Case-2 : With friction $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{a}=\mathrm{g} \sin \theta-\mu \mathrm{g} \cos \theta \\ & \ell=\frac{1}{2}(\mathrm{~g} \sin \theta-\mu \mathrm{g} \cos \theta) \mathrm{t}_2^2 \\ & \sqrt{\frac{2 \ell}{\mathrm{g} \sin \theta-\mu g \cos \theta}}=\mathrm{n} \sqrt{\frac{2 \ell}{\mathrm{g} \sin \theta}} \\ & \mu=1-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}^2} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A block of mass 5 kg is placed on a rough inclined surface as shown in the figure. If F→1 is the force required to just move the block up the inclined plane and F→2 is the force required to just prevent the block from sliding down, then the value of F→1−F→2 is: [Use g=10 m s-2]
question: A block of mass 5 kg is placed on a rough inclined surface as shown in the figure. If F→1 is the force required to just move the block up the inclined plane and F→2 is the force required to just prevent the block from sliding down, then the value of F→1−F→2 is: [Use g=10 m s-2]
solution:
Limiting friction force is, fmax=μmg cosθ

=0.1×50×32

=2.53 N

For first case:

We can write, F1=mg sinθ+fmax

=25+2.53

For second case:

F2=mg sinθ−fmax

=25−2.53

∴F1−F2=53 N

subject: Physics
q: At any instant the velocity of a particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>t</mi><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mo>+</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . If the force acting on the particle at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mo>+</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> . Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be:
question: At any instant the velocity of a particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>t</mi><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mo>+</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . If the force acting on the particle at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mo>+</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> . Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be:
solution:
The equation for velocity is 

v→=2t i^ + 3t2 j^

Thus, the acceleration is 

a→=dv→dt=d(2t i^+3t2 j^)dt=2 i^+6t j^

At t=1 s, a→=2 i^ + 6 j^

The force is written as F→=ma→=2 i^+6 j^2=i^ +3 j^

Comparing it with the given value of force, x=3.

subject: Physics
q: A car of $800 \mathrm{~kg}$ is taking turn on a banked road of radius $300 \mathrm{~m}$ and angle of banking $30^{\circ}$. If coefficient of static friction is 0.2 then the maximum speed with which car can negotiate the turn safely: $\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2, \sqrt{3}=1.73\right)$
question: A car of $800 \mathrm{~kg}$ is taking turn on a banked road of radius $300 \mathrm{~m}$ and angle of banking $30^{\circ}$. If coefficient of static friction is 0.2 then the maximum speed with which car can negotiate the turn safely: $\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2, \sqrt{3}=1.73\right)$
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{m}=800 \mathrm{~kg} \\ & \mathrm{r}=300 \mathrm{~m} \\ & \theta=30^{\circ} \\ & \mu_{\mathrm{s}}=0.2 \\ & \mathrm{~V}_{\max }=\sqrt{\operatorname{Rg}\left[\frac{\tan \theta+\mu}{1-\mu \tan \theta}\right]} \\ & =\sqrt{300 \times \mathrm{g} \times\left[\frac{\tan 30^{\circ}+0.2}{1-0.2 \times \tan 30^{\circ}}\right]} \\ & =\sqrt{300 \times 10 \times\left[\frac{0.57+0.2}{1-0.2 \times 0.57}\right]} \\ & \mathrm{V}_{\max }=51.4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A ball is released from rest from point P of a smooth semi-spherical vessel as shown in figure. The ratio of the centripetal force and normal reaction on the ball at point Q is A while angular position of point Q is α with respect to point P. Which of the following graphs represent the correct relation between A and α when ball goes from Q to R?
question: A ball is released from rest from point P of a smooth semi-spherical vessel as shown in figure. The ratio of the centripetal force and normal reaction on the ball at point Q is A while angular position of point Q is α with respect to point P. Which of the following graphs represent the correct relation between A and α when ball goes from Q to R?
solution:
At angular position α as shown in the figure, applying conservation of mechanical energy

mgRsinα=12mv2⇒mv2R=2mgsinα

In the radial direction,

N-mgsinα=mv2R⇒N=3mgsinα

Now the ratio of centripetal force and normal reaction is,

A=mv2RN=2mgsinα3mgsinα=23.  Therefore, A is constant.

subject: Physics
q: For a free body diagram shown in the figure, the four forces are applied in the 'x' and 'y' directions. What additional force must be applied and at what angle with positive x-axis so that the net acceleration of body is zero?
question: For a free body diagram shown in the figure, the four forces are applied in the 'x' and 'y' directions. What additional force must be applied and at what angle with positive x-axis so that the net acceleration of body is zero?
solution:
Let additional force required be F→.

Vector sum of the four forces and additional force.

F→+5i^-6i^+7j^-8j^=0

Force required to make net acceleration zero

F→=i^+j^

Therefore, the required force is F→=12+12=2 N

Angle with x-axis: tanθ=y component x component =11

Thus, θ=45°

subject: Physics
q: Two masses M1 and M2 are tied together at the two ends of a light inextensible string that passes over a frictionless pulley. When the mass M2 is twice that of M1. the acceleration of the system is a1. When the mass M2 is thrice that of M1. The acceleration of The system is a2. The ratio a1a2 will be
question: Two masses M1 and M2 are tied together at the two ends of a light inextensible string that passes over a frictionless pulley. When the mass M2 is twice that of M1. the acceleration of the system is a1. When the mass M2 is thrice that of M1. The acceleration of The system is a2. The ratio a1a2 will be
solution:
In both cases m1>m2. Therefore, m1 will accelerate downward and m2 will accelerate upward.

For m1,

m1g-T=m1a

For m2,

T-m2g=m2a

Adding the above equations we get,

a=m1-m2m1+m2g

⇒a1=m1-m2m1+m2g=2m2-m22m2+m2g=g3

 and

⇒a2=m1-m2m1+m2g=3m2-m23m2+m2g=g2

⇒a1a2=23

subject: Physics
q: One end of a massless spring of spring constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> and natural length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is fixed while the other end is connected to a small object of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> lying on a frictionless table. The spring remains horizontal on the table. If the object is made to rotate at an angular velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ω</mi></math> about an axis passing trough fixed end, then the elongation of the spring will be
question: One end of a massless spring of spring constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> and natural length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is fixed while the other end is connected to a small object of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> lying on a frictionless table. The spring remains horizontal on the table. If the object is made to rotate at an angular velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ω</mi></math> about an axis passing trough fixed end, then the elongation of the spring will be
solution:
The particle is moving in a horizontal circle, so it is accelerated towards the centre with magnitude v2r. The horizontal force on the particle is due to the spring and is given by kx, where x is the elongation and k is the spring constant.

kx=mv2r=mω2r=mω2l0+x

⇒k-mω2x=mω2l0

⇒x=mω2l0k-mω2

subject: Physics
q:
This question has Statement 1 and Statement 2 . Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement 1: If you push on a cart being pulled by a horse so that it does not move, the cart pushes you back with an equal and opposite force.
Statement 2: The cart does not move because the force described in statement 1 cancel each other.
question:
This question has Statement 1 and Statement 2 . Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement 1: If you push on a cart being pulled by a horse so that it does not move, the cart pushes you back with an equal and opposite force.
Statement 2: The cart does not move because the force described in statement 1 cancel each other.
options: Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is the correct explanation of Statement 1.
Statement 1 is false, Statement 2 is true.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is false.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is not the correct explanation of Statement 1.
solution: According to newton third law of motion i.e. every action is associated with equal and opposite reaction.

subject: Physics
q: A coin is placed on a disc. The coefficient of friction between the coin and the disc is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>μ</mi></math>. If the distance of the coin from the center of the disc is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>, the maximum angular velocity which can be given to the disc, so that the coin does not slip away, is :
question: A coin is placed on a disc. The coefficient of friction between the coin and the disc is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>μ</mi></math>. If the distance of the coin from the center of the disc is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>, the maximum angular velocity which can be given to the disc, so that the coin does not slip away, is :
solution:
Normal force acting on the coin is, N=mg.

Required friction force to provide centripetal force for the coin is, f=mω2r and maximum value of static friction force available is, f=μN=μmg.

For limiting condition, we can write

⇒μmg=mrω2

⇒ω=μgr

subject: Physics
q: A particle is moving with uniform speed along the circumference of a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> under the action of a central fictitious force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> which is inversely proportional to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>R</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math>. Its time period of revolution will be given by :
question: A particle is moving with uniform speed along the circumference of a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> under the action of a central fictitious force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> which is inversely proportional to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>R</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math>. Its time period of revolution will be given by :
solution:
F∝1R3

KR3=mω2R

ω2=Km×1R4

2πT2=Km×1R4

T2∝R4

T∝R2

subject: Physics
q: The coefficient of static friction between a wooden block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and a vertical rough wall is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo></math> The magnitude of the horizontal force that should be applied on the block to keep it adhere to the wall will be______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="⌈" close="]"><mrow><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: The coefficient of static friction between a wooden block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and a vertical rough wall is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo></math> The magnitude of the horizontal force that should be applied on the block to keep it adhere to the wall will be______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="⌈" close="]"><mrow><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
F.B.D. of the block is shown in the diagram

Since block is at rest therefore

fr-mg=0             ...1

F-N=0              ...2

fr≤μN

In limiting case

fr=μN=μF           …3

Using eq. 1 and 3

∴  μF=mg

⇒F=0.5×100.2=25 N

subject: Physics
q: Consider a block and trolley system as shown in figure. If the coefficient of kinetic friction between the trolley and the surface is 0.04, the acceleration of the system in m s-2 is: (Consider that the string is massless and unstretchable and the pulley is also massless and frictionless) :
question: Consider a block and trolley system as shown in figure. If the coefficient of kinetic friction between the trolley and the surface is 0.04, the acceleration of the system in m s-2 is: (Consider that the string is massless and unstretchable and the pulley is also massless and frictionless) :
solution:
Maximum kinetic friction acting, fk=μN=0.04×20g=8 Newton

Therefore, acceleration of the system will bea=60−826=2 m s-2

subject: Physics
q: A 4 kg mass moves under the influence of a force $\vec{F}=\left(4 t^{3} \hat{i}-3 t \hat{j}\right) \mathrm{N}$ where $t$ is the time in second. If mass starts from origin at $t=0$, the velocity and position after $t=2 \mathrm{~s}$ will be:
question: A 4 kg mass moves under the influence of a force $\vec{F}=\left(4 t^{3} \hat{i}-3 t \hat{j}\right) \mathrm{N}$ where $t$ is the time in second. If mass starts from origin at $t=0$, the velocity and position after $t=2 \mathrm{~s}$ will be:
solution:
Given $\vec{F} = (4t^3\hat{i} - 3t\hat{j})$ N, m = 4 kg
$\vec{a} = \frac{\vec{F}}{m} = t^3\hat{i} - \frac{3t}{4}\hat{j}$
Integrating with $\vec{v}(0) = 0$:
$\vec{v} = \frac{t^4}{4}\hat{i} - \frac{3t^2}{8}\hat{j}$
At $t = 2$: $\vec{v} = \frac{16}{4}\hat{i} - \frac{12}{8}\hat{j} = 4\hat{i} - \frac{3}{2}\hat{j}$
Integrating with $\vec{r}(0) = 0$:
$\vec{r} = \frac{t^5}{20}\hat{i} - \frac{t^3}{8}\hat{j}$
At $t = 2$: $\vec{r} = \frac{32}{20}\hat{i} - \frac{8}{8}\hat{j} = \frac{8}{5}\hat{i} - \hat{j}$

subject: Physics
q:
A hanging mass M is connected to a four times bigger mass by using a string pulley arrangement, as shown in the figure. The bigger mass is placed on a horizontal ice-slab and being pulled by 2Mg force. In this situation, tension in the string is x5Mg for x=_____. Neglect mass of the string and friction of the block (bigger mass) with ice slab.
(Given g= acceleration due to gravity)
question:
A hanging mass M is connected to a four times bigger mass by using a string pulley arrangement, as shown in the figure. The bigger mass is placed on a horizontal ice-slab and being pulled by 2Mg force. In this situation, tension in the string is x5Mg for x=_____. Neglect mass of the string and friction of the block (bigger mass) with ice slab.
(Given g= acceleration due to gravity)
solution:
Equation for 4M: 2Mg-T=4Ma   ...1

Equation for M: T-Mg=Ma   ...2

Adding both equation we get,

Mg=5Ma ⇒a=g5

Now, tension T=Mg+Ma=6Mg5

Therefore, x=6

subject: Physics
q: A block is kept on a frictionless inclined surface with angle of inclination $\alpha$. The incline is given an acceleration a to keep the block stationary. Then a is equal to
question: A block is kept on a frictionless inclined surface with angle of inclination $\alpha$. The incline is given an acceleration a to keep the block stationary. Then a is equal to
solution: $m g \sin \alpha=m a \cos \alpha$ $\therefore \mathrm{a}=\mathrm{g} \tan \alpha$

subject: Physics
q: Consider a uniform cubical box of side a on a rough floor that is to be moved by applying minimum possible force F at a point b above its centre of mass (see figure). If the coefficient of friction is μ=0.4 , the maximum possible value of 100×ba for a box not to topple before moving is ________
question: Consider a uniform cubical box of side a on a rough floor that is to be moved by applying minimum possible force F at a point b above its centre of mass (see figure). If the coefficient of friction is μ=0.4 , the maximum possible value of 100×ba for a box not to topple before moving is ________
solution:
For no toppling
Fa2+b≤mga2
μa2+μb≤a2
0.2a+0.4b≤0.5a
0.4b≤0.3a
b≤3a2
b≤0.75a (in limiting case)
But it is not possible as b can maximum be equal to 0.5a
∴100bamax=50.00

subject: Physics
q: A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is placed on a surface having vertical cross section given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math>. If coefficient of friction is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math>, the maximum height above the ground at which block can be placed without slipping is:
question: A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is placed on a surface having vertical cross section given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math>. If coefficient of friction is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math>, the maximum height above the ground at which block can be placed without slipping is:
solution:
At equilibrium, tanθ=μ.

Now, tanθ is slope of the curve. Therefore,

tanθ=dydx=x2

Now, 

x2=0.5 ⇒x=1

Required height 

y=x24=14 m.

subject: Physics
q:
Consider a frame that is made up of two thin massless rods AB and AC as shown in the figure. A vertical force P→ of magnitude 100 N is applied at point A of the frame.

Suppose the force is P→ resolved parallel to the arms AB and AC of the frame. The magnitude of the resolved component along the arm AC is xN. The value of x, to the nearest integer, is ________.

[Given : sin35°=0.573,cos35°=0.819, sin110°=0.939,cos110°=-0.342]
question:
Consider a frame that is made up of two thin massless rods AB and AC as shown in the figure. A vertical force P→ of magnitude 100 N is applied at point A of the frame.

Suppose the force is P→ resolved parallel to the arms AB and AC of the frame. The magnitude of the resolved component along the arm AC is xN. The value of x, to the nearest integer, is ________.

[Given : sin35°=0.573,cos35°=0.819, sin110°=0.939,cos110°=-0.342]
solution:
Component along AC

=100cos35° N

=100×0.819 N

=81.9 N

≈82 N

subject: Physics
q: A smooth block is released at rest on a $45^{\circ}$ incline and then slides a distance d. The time taken to slide is $\mathrm{n}$ times as much to slide on rough incline than on a smooth incline. The coefficient of friction is
question: A smooth block is released at rest on a $45^{\circ}$ incline and then slides a distance d. The time taken to slide is $\mathrm{n}$ times as much to slide on rough incline than on a smooth incline. The coefficient of friction is
solution:
$\mathrm{d}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{g}}{\sqrt{2}} \mathrm{t}_1^2$
$\mathrm{~d}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{g}}{\sqrt{2}}\left(1-\mu_{\mathrm{k}}\right) \mathrm{t}_2^2$
$\frac{\mathrm{t}_2^2}{\mathrm{t}_1^2}=\mathrm{n}^2=\frac{1}{1-\mu_{\mathrm{k}}}$

subject: Physics
q:
In the given figure the blocks $A, B$ and $C$ weigh $4 \mathrm{~kg}, 6 \mathrm{~kg}$ and 8 kg respectively. The co-efficient of sliding friction between any two surfaces is 0.5. The force $\vec{F}$ required to slide the block $C$ with constant speed is $\_\_\_\_$ N.
(Use $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$)
question:
In the given figure the blocks $A, B$ and $C$ weigh $4 \mathrm{~kg}, 6 \mathrm{~kg}$ and 8 kg respectively. The co-efficient of sliding friction between any two surfaces is 0.5. The force $\vec{F}$ required to slide the block $C$ with constant speed is $\_\_\_\_$ N.
(Use $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$)
solution:
For the 8 kg block (C) to move with constant velocity, $F_{net} = 0$.

Friction forces acting:
- Between C and ground: $f = \mu(m_A + m_B + m_C)g = 0.5 \times (4+6+8) \times 10 = 90$ N
- Between C and B: $f = \mu(m_A + m_B)g = 0.5 \times (4+6) \times 10 = 50$ N

For the 6 kg block (B):
$T = f_{\text{B on ground}} + f_{\text{A on B}}$
$T = \mu(m_A + m_B)g \cdot \frac{m_B}{m_A+m_B} + \mu \cdot m_A \cdot g$
$T = 20 + 50 = 70$ N

For the 8 kg block (C):
$F = 90 + T + 50 = 90 + 70 + 50 = 210$ N

subject: Physics
q: The time taken by a block of mass $m$ to slide down from the highest point to the lowest point on a rough inclined plane is $50\%$ more compared to the time taken by the same block on identical inclined smooth plane. Both inclined planes are at $45^\circ$ with the horizontal. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the rough inclined surface and block is _____.
question: The time taken by a block of mass $m$ to slide down from the highest point to the lowest point on a rough inclined plane is $50\%$ more compared to the time taken by the same block on identical inclined smooth plane. Both inclined planes are at $45^\circ$ with the horizontal. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the rough inclined surface and block is _____.
solution:
Let the length of the inclined plane be $s$.

For the smooth inclined plane, the acceleration of the block is $a_1 = g \sin \theta$.

The time taken to slide down is $t_1 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2s}{a_1}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2s}{g \sin \theta}}$.

For the rough inclined plane, the acceleration of the block is $a_2 = g \sin \theta - \mu_k g \cos \theta$.

The time taken to slide down is $t_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2s}{a_2}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2s}{g (\sin \theta - \mu_k \cos \theta)}}$.

Given that $t_2$ is $50\%$ more than $t_1$, we have $t_2 = t_1 + 0.5 t_1 = \dfrac{3}{2} t_1$.

Squaring both sides gives $t_2^2 = \dfrac{9}{4} t_1^2$.

Substituting the expressions for $t_1^2$ and $t_2^2$:

$\dfrac{2s}{g (\sin \theta - \mu_k \cos \theta)} = \dfrac{9}{4} \left( \dfrac{2s}{g \sin \theta} \right)$

$\dfrac{1}{\sin \theta - \mu_k \cos \theta} = \dfrac{9}{4 \sin \theta}$

$4 \sin \theta = 9 \sin \theta - 9 \mu_k \cos \theta$

$9 \mu_k \cos \theta = 5 \sin \theta$

$\mu_k = \dfrac{5}{9} \tan \theta$

Given $\theta = 45^\circ$, we have $\tan 45^\circ = 1$.

$\mu_k = \dfrac{5}{9} \times 1 = \dfrac{5}{9}$

Answer: $5/9$

solution:
F=Rt2vt⇒mdvdt=Rt2vt

∫dvv=∫Rdtmt2

ln v=-Rmt

∴  ln v∝1t

subject: Physics
q:
A steel block of 10 kg rests on a horizontal floor as shown. When three iron cylinders are placed on it as shown, the block and cylinders go down with an acceleration 0.2 m s-2. The normal reaction R' by the floor if mass of the iron cylinders are equal and of 20 kg each is in N,

[Take g=10 m s-2 and μs=0.2
question:
A steel block of 10 kg rests on a horizontal floor as shown. When three iron cylinders are placed on it as shown, the block and cylinders go down with an acceleration 0.2 m s-2. The normal reaction R' by the floor if mass of the iron cylinders are equal and of 20 kg each is in N,

[Take g=10 m s-2 and μs=0.2
solution:
Writing force equation in vertical direction, 
Mg-N=Ma
⇒70 g-N=70×0.2
⇒N=70[ g-0.2]=70×9.8
∴N=686 newton
Note: Since there is no compressive normal from the sides, hence friction will not act.
Hence option 2.

subject: Physics
q:
Three masses $m_1 = 4$ kg, $m_2 = 4$ kg and $m_3 = 6$ kg are suspended from a fixed smooth frictionless pulley as shown in the figure below. The value of $T_1/T_2$ is _____.
(take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
question:
Three masses $m_1 = 4$ kg, $m_2 = 4$ kg and $m_3 = 6$ kg are suspended from a fixed smooth frictionless pulley as shown in the figure below. The value of $T_1/T_2$ is _____.
(take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
solution:
Let the acceleration of the system be $a$. The total mass on the right side is $m_2 + m_3 = 4 + 6 = 10$ kg, and the mass on the left side is $m_1 = 4$ kg. Since $10$ kg $> 4$ kg, the right side accelerates downwards and the left side accelerates upwards.

The common acceleration $a$ of the system is given by:
$a = \dfrac{(m_2 + m_3) - m_1}{m_1 + m_2 + m_3} g$
$a = \dfrac{10 - 4}{4 + 4 + 6} g = \dfrac{6}{14} g = \dfrac{3}{7} g$

For mass $m_1$, the equation of motion is:
$T_1 - m_1 g = m_1 a$
$T_1 = m_1(g + a) = 4 \left(g + \dfrac{3}{7}g\right) = 4 \left(\dfrac{10}{7}g\right) = \dfrac{40}{7}g$

For mass $m_3$, the equation of motion is:
$m_3 g - T_2 = m_3 a$
$T_2 = m_3(g - a) = 6 \left(g - \dfrac{3}{7}g\right) = 6 \left(\dfrac{4}{7}g\right) = \dfrac{24}{7}g$

The ratio of the tensions is:
$\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = \dfrac{\dfrac{40}{7}g}{\dfrac{24}{7}g} = \dfrac{40}{24} = \dfrac{5}{3}$

Answer: $5/3$

subject: Physics
q: A mass m is attached to two springs as shown in figure. The spring constants of two springs are K1 and K2. For the frictionless surface, the time period of oscillation of mass m is
question: A mass m is attached to two springs as shown in figure. The spring constants of two springs are K1 and K2. For the frictionless surface, the time period of oscillation of mass m is
solution:
For the given combination, if the block is displaced towards right, elongation in one spring will be equal to the compression in other and the direction of force due to both spring will be the same and restoring in nature. Hence, both springs are effectively in parallel combination.

Therefore,

⇒Keff=K1+K2 and

⇒T=2πmKeff=2πmK1+K2

question: A block of mass m is kept on a platform which starts from rest with a constant acceleration g/2 upwards, as shown in the figure. Work done by normal reaction on block in time t is

subject: Physics
q: A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is placed at rest on a table of rough surface. Now, if a force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> is applied in the direction parallel to surface of the table, the block slides through a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> in an interval of time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. Coefficient of kinetic friction is (given, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> ):
question: A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is placed at rest on a table of rough surface. Now, if a force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> is applied in the direction parallel to surface of the table, the block slides through a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> in an interval of time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. Coefficient of kinetic friction is (given, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> ):
solution:
Forces acting on block is shown below

From the second equation of motion,

s=12at2⇒50=12a×102⇒a=1 m s-2

Applying Newton's second law in vertical direction,

R-mg=0⇒R=mg

(where R is the magnitude of normal reaction on the block due to the surface)

If μ is the coefficient of kinetic friction between the block and the surface, applying Newton's second law in horizontal direction,

30-μR=ma⇒30-μmg=ma⇒30-50μ=5⇒μ=12=0.5

subject: Physics
q: A uniform chain of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> length is placed on a table such that a part of its length is hanging over the edge of the table. The system is at rest. The co-efficient of static friction between the chain and the surface of the table is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math>, the maximum length of the chain hanging from the table is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
question: A uniform chain of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> length is placed on a table such that a part of its length is hanging over the edge of the table. The system is at rest. The co-efficient of static friction between the chain and the surface of the table is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math>, the maximum length of the chain hanging from the table is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
solution:
Let, λ=mass per unit length of the chain and length of mass hanging through the table is x.

Normal force applied by the table on the chain will be, N=l-xλg.

Tension developed in the chain will balance the gravitational force acting in the downward direction, T=λxg.

Maximum limiting force possible for the part of the table, fmax=μN=μl-xλg

For equilibrium,

fmax=T,

⇒μl-xλg=λxg⇒12×6-x=x ⇒x=2 m

subject: Physics
q: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> experiences two forces <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi>F</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>8</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>7</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi>F</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>.</mo></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> The acceleration acting on the body is:
question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> experiences two forces <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi>F</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>8</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>7</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi>F</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>.</mo></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> The acceleration acting on the body is:
solution:
Net force F→=F→1+F→2=8i^+4j^+4k^

Therefore, required acceleration a→=F→m=2i^+j^+k^
